note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; pronunciation spellings kept exactly as written, e.g. 'C-R-M', 'T-C-P-A', 'SLA'). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) puts the s13 end at 256.629s, which overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (256.209s) by 0.420s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the master was joined with 12 inter-block gaps of ~0.36s each (avg 0.398s) and NO trailing gap, so the +0.4-per-block formula accumulates a spurious 13th gap. Onsets were therefore measured by normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-12-sNN.mp3 against the joined master (8 kHz mono PCM); all 13 blocks matched at score >= 0.9999. Master ffprobe duration 256.209s.

phrases.text: Start in Settings, under AI Agent, and open Conversations.
This is the customer facing agent, the one your customers text and talk to.
Not Ask Shyne in the header. That's a staff tool, and a different playbook.
The transcript is the ticket, so this log is always your first stop.
Read the layout before you search.
Six KPIs across the top cover thirty days.
Below them, a table: customer, channel, topic, status, message count, sentiment.
Today's demo tenant shows no conversations at all, while Analytics reports thousands.
That gap is a demo artifact. Trust this log only after it actually loads.
Now narrow to the chat in question.
Search or filter on the customer, the channel, or the topic.
The message count tells you how long the exchange dragged on,
and sentiment tells you how it felt.
Opening the row gives you the full back and forth plus the conversation ID.
That ID rides along into your ticket.
Next question: did it hand off when it should have? Click Escalations.
Seven open, three claimed, one SLA breach.
Average wait, six minutes and change.
This queue is where the agent admits it needs a human.
The reason column is the diagnostic.
Negative sentiment on a billing dispute. A refund above the cap.
A customer asking for a manager. Eight turns with no resolution.
Those are the guardrails, and a healthy agent trips them.
If your customer's chat sits here with a sensible reason, the agent behaved.
If it never showed up, that gap is the misbehavior you report.
So where does a handoff land?
In the C-R-M module, under Service Desk, open the Agent Console.
This is the human side of the escalation you just read.
A teammate sets themselves available and picks the case up right here.
Look over the queue controls.
Filters for mine, unassigned, team, and all. Channel chips.
Sort by longest wait or by priority.
Today the queue is empty, so the console panes stay blank.
That's fine. You came to confirm the intake exists, not to claim anything.
Now chase the bad answer. Back in Settings, open the Knowledge Base.
These are the documents the agent searches before it answers a customer.
A wrong answer usually starts here, with an article that went stale or never existed at all.
Read the table, not just the counts.
Status tells you whether an article is indexed and searchable.
Updated tells you how old it is.
Search the topic the customer asked about,
and if nothing comes back, you have probably found your cause.
Leave the add and re-index controls alone. Each one changes the index.
Channel next. Open Channels and Deployment.
Three channels, three of three live.
Thirty day uptime just shy of one hundred percent, latency around four hundred milliseconds.
Healthy numbers up top. The story is in the cards below.
Read all three cards, and touch none of the test controls.
Web chat is installed and live.
SMS is live, with stop and help handled automatically.
Voice is beta, and beta means rougher behavior is expected, so say so up front.
And that nighttime silence complaint? Quiet hours.
The overview page flags T-C-P-A quiet hours on for SMS.
The agent wasn't ignoring anyone. It was honoring the window.
One more page. Analytics.
Thirty eight hundred conversations in thirty days,
and seventy eight percent never needed a human.
That is containment.
Web runs highest, voice lowest, which fits a beta channel.
A channel whose containment has slipped is real evidence the agent is struggling there,
and it belongs in your escalation.
Now write the ticket and stop.
The conversation ID. The channel. The containment context.
Then the finding: a missing escalation row, a stale article,
or a channel state like voice beta or quiet hours.
You read six screens and changed nothing.
Configuration and knowledge edits happen upstream, with the right role.
Your job was the diagnosis, and it's done.
